content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve ever built a website or application, you’ve probably spent more time searching for an icon than you expected.
It usually starts with a simple task. You need a search icon, a settings icon, or maybe a calendar icon. You open your favorite icon website, search for it, and realize it doesn’t have the style you’re looking for. Then you open another website. Then another. Before you know it, you have several tabs open just to find a single SVG icon.
I used to do the same thing.
It wasn’t difficult, but it interrupted my workflow every single day. Even if finding one icon only took a few minutes, those minutes quickly added up across multiple projects.
Eventually, I changed the way I search for icons, and now I usually find the right SVG icon in under 30 seconds.
The problem with searching for SVG icons
There are hundreds of excellent open source icon libraries available today. Libraries like Lucide, Heroicons, Tabler Icons, Phosphor Icons, Remix Icon, Material Symbols, Bootstrap Icons, and many others all offer beautiful icons.
The problem isn’t the lack of icons.
The problem is that every library has its own website, search system, categories, and download process.
A typical workflow looks something like this:

• Search on one website.

• Don’t find the icon.

• Open another website.

• Search again.

• Compare styles.

• Download the icon.

• Repeat for the next icon.
When you’re building user interfaces every day, this becomes surprisingly repetitive.
What I wanted instead
I wanted something much simpler.
Instead of remembering dozens of icon websites, I wanted one place where I could search everything at once.
I also wanted to compare different icon styles without opening multiple tabs.
Most importantly, I wanted to spend less time searching and more time building.
That’s when I started using a different workflow.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

My workflow for finding icons
Now my process is very simple.
Step 1: Search once
Instead of opening multiple websites, I search once using a keyword like:

• user

• search

• calendar

• folder

• home

• notification

• arrow

• settings
Within a few seconds I can see results from hundreds of icon libraries.
Step 2: Compare different styles
Sometimes an outlined icon looks better.
Sometimes a filled icon fits the design.
Sometimes I need something minimal.
Instead of visiting different websites to compare styles, I can view multiple options in one place and decide which one works best.
This alone saves a surprising amount of time.
Step 3: Preview the icon
Before downloading, I quickly preview the icon to make sure it matches the rest of my interface.
If it doesn’t, I simply choose another one.
There’s no need to go back and search another website.
Step 4: Download or copy
Once I find the icon I want, I simply download it or copy it into my project.
The entire process usually takes less than 30 seconds.
Why this workflow is faster
After using this approach for several projects, I noticed a few advantages.
No more opening multiple websites
One search replaces several browser tabs.
Easier comparison
Since different icon libraries appear together, it’s much easier to compare styles before making a decision.
Better focus
One thing I didn’t expect was how much this improved my concentration.
Every time you switch websites, search again, and wait for another page to load, you lose focus on what you were building.
Keeping everything in one place makes it easier to stay in the flow.
Faster development
Finding icons isn’t the biggest part of building software, but it’s something almost every developer does repeatedly.
Saving even a few minutes each day becomes significant over weeks and months.
The tool I use
The tool I use for this workflow is IconShelf.
Instead of maintaining another icon library, IconShelf lets me search across more than 200 icon libraries from a single search box.
When I search for an icon, I can immediately browse different styles, preview the results, and download the one that fits my project.
Since everything is available in one place, I rarely need to visit individual icon websites anymore.
Here’s a quick 45-second demo of the workflow I use every day.
It also fits into my development workflow
One thing I appreciate is that I don’t always have to leave my editor.
Besides the website, IconShelf is also available as a Chrome Extension and a VS Code Extension.
That means I can search for icons while browsing documentation or even while writing code, without constantly switching between applications.
It’s a small improvement, but it helps keep my workflow uninterrupted.
Who this workflow is useful for
This approach is useful if you regularly build:

• Websites

• React applications

• Vue applications

• SaaS products

• Dashboards

• Mobile apps

• Landing pages

• Design systems
If your work involves icons every day, reducing the time spent searching quickly becomes noticeable.
Final thoughts
Searching for SVG icons isn’t a difficult task, but it is one of those small activities that quietly consumes time throughout the day.
For me, the biggest improvement wasn’t finding better icons, it was simplifying the entire process.
Instead of remembering dozens of icon websites, opening multiple tabs, and repeating the same search over and over, I now search once, compare the results, and continue building.
It may only save a few minutes at a time, but over the course of a project, those minutes add up.
Sometimes improving your workflow isn’t about learning a new framework or writing better code. Sometimes it’s simply about removing one small source of friction that you encounter every single day.
